1: bayyāmîm hāhēm ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl ûḇayyāmîm hāhēm šēḇeṭ haddānî məḇaqquēš-lô nāḥălāh lāšeḇet kî lōʾ-nāpəlāh lô ʿaḏ hayyôm hahûʾ bəṯôk šibṭê yiśrāʾēl bənāḥălāh — 'In those days there was no king in Israel, and in those days the tribe of Dan was seeking for itself an inheritance (nāḥălāh) to live in, for to that day no inheritance had fallen to them among the tribes of Israel.' The chapter opens with the double refrain (ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl) and immediately maps its consequence: without covenant order, even territorial inheritance breaks down. nāḥălāh (H5159, inheritance — the covenantal land-gift, what YHWH promised and Joshua distributed) — Dan's failure to secure their allotment (Josh 19:40-48; cf. Judg 1:34 — the Amorites pushed them back) means they exist as a landless tribe, contrary to covenant purpose. Their solution (migration north) is presented without divine sanction.
2: wayyišləḥû bənê-ḏān mimmišpaḥtām ḥămiššāh ʾănāšîm miqṣôtām ʾanšê bənê-ḥayil — 'The sons of Dan sent from their clan five men from their extremities, men of valor.' miqqəṣôtām (from their extremities/fringes — the whole tribe represented by men from the outer edges) — the extremity language suggests comprehensive representation. ʾanšê bənê-ḥayil (H2428 H1121, men of valor/capability) — the same phrase used of Boaz (Ruth 2:1) and Gideon's greeting (Judg 6:12). The reconnaissance mission echoes the Exodus-era spies (Num 13), but without Moses's authorization or YHWH's command — a structural parallel that underscores the absence of divine guidance.
6: wayyōʾmer lāhem hakkōhēn ləkû ləšālôm nekōaḥ YHWH darkəkem ʾăšer tēlkû-ḇāh — 'The priest said to them: Go in peace. The way in which you go is before YHWH.' The Levite-priest pronounces an oracle of favorable outcome — but the text provides no indication that he actually inquired of YHWH, nor is the ʾêpôḏ or tərāpîm (v5 — 'they inquired of God' via Micah's unauthorized shrine) a legitimate instrument of divine revelation. nekōaḥ YHWH (before/in the sight of YHWH) is the priestly blessing formula used to validate the journey — but the entire apparatus (private shrine, private Levite, idols) is corrupt. The oracle may be technically fulfilled, yet its source is polluted.
7: wayyēlkû ḥămiššāh hāʾănāšîm wayyāḇōʾû lāyišāh wayyirʾû ʾet-hāʿām ʾăšer-bəqirḇāh yōšeḇet lāḇeṭaḥ — 'The five men went and came to Laish and saw the people who were in it, dwelling in security (yōšeḇet lāḇeṭaḥ).' lāḇeṭaḥ (H983, in security/trust — the state of dwelling without fear of attack) — this word is used of Israel dwelling securely under YHWH's protection (Deut 33:12,28; Judg 8:11). The people of Laish are described with four vulnerability markers: dwelling securely, manner of Sidon (peaceful/commercial not military), quiet and unsuspecting (šōqēṭ wəḇōṭēaḥ), no lack of anything, far from Sidon and from anyone who might rescue them (miyyad mêṣar — no deliverer). This accumulation of vulnerability terms prepares the reader to be troubled, not cheered, by what follows.
14: wayaʿănû ḥămiššāh hāʾănāšîm hahōlkîm lərāggēl ʾet-ʾereṣ lāyiš wayyōʾmərû ʾel-ʾăḥêhem — 'Then the five men who went to spy out the land of Laish answered and said to their brothers.' The men know Micah's house contains a pesel ûmassēkāh ʾêpôḏ ûtərāpîm (graven image, molten image, ephod, and teraphim) and direct the tribe to take them. The casual recommendation to steal a shrine's sacred objects — instruments of covenant violation — is presented as pragmatic military intelligence, not sacrilege. That 600 armed men will stop to plunder a private household's religious objects on their way to conquer a city reveals the total inversion of values that marks the book's closing chapters.
19: wayyōʾmərû lô ḥăraš śîm-yāḏəkā ʿal-pîkā ûlēk ʿimmānû wihyēh-lānû ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn — 'They said to him: Be silent; put your hand on your mouth, and come with us and be to us a father and a priest.' ḥărēš (H2790, be silent) — the command to silence is coercive: be quiet about this theft. ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn (father and priest) — the Levite will gain a tribe-level priestly role rather than a household one. His ready acceptance (wayyîṭaḇ lēḇ hakkōhēn — the priest's heart was glad, v20) reveals his mercenary character: priesthood is portable and negotiable, not a covenant vocation. The offer parodies legitimate Levitical ministry: Aaron was 'mouth and priest' to Moses; this Levite becomes 'priest' to stolen idols.
27: wəhēmmāh lāqəḥû ʾēt ʾăšer-ʿāśāh mîkāh wəʾet-hakkōhēn ʾăšer hāyāh-lô wayyāḇōʾû ʿal-lāyiš ʿal-ʿam šōqēṭ ûḇōṭēaḥ wayyakkû ʾôtām ləpî-ḥāreḇ — 'They took what Micah had made, and the priest who had been his, and came to Laish, to a people quiet and unsuspecting, and struck them with the edge of the sword.' ḥāreḇ (H2719, sword) — the conquest of Laish with the sword echoes the Canaanite conquests of Joshua, but without YHWH's command, without legitimate territory, against a peaceful people. ʾên maṣṣîl (H5337, no one delivering/rescuing) — Laish had no deliverer, as the spies noted (v28). The sad irony: Israel, who was delivered from Egypt by YHWH when no human could save them, now acts as the unchecked oppressor of an undefended people.
30: wayyāqîmû lāhem bənê-ḏān ʾet-happāsel wayyōnāṯān ben-gərōšōm ben-mōšeh hûʾ ûḇānāyw hāyû kōhănîm laššēḇeṭ haddānî — 'The sons of Dan set up the carved image for themselves, and Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses, he and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan.' The genealogical bombshell: the anonymous 'Levite' of chs17-18 is revealed as Jonathan son of Gershom son of Moses — Moses's own grandson is the first idolatrous priest of Danite Israel. Some manuscripts read mənašše (Manasseh) with a superscripted nun — a scribal protective alteration to spare Moses the shame. But the earliest tradition (and the Qere) reads mōšeh: Moses's grandson. The priestly dynasty of Micah's idolatrous shrine serves Dan ʿaḏ yôm gəlôt hāʾāreṣ — until the day of the land's exile (Assyrian conquest, 722 BCE).
31: wayyāśîmû lāhem ʾet-pesel mîkāh ʾăšer ʿāśāh kol-yəmê heyôt bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm bəšilōh — 'They set up Micah's carved image that he made, all the days that the house of God was at Shiloh.' The conclusion is devastating: while the legitimate tabernacle stood at Shiloh (bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm bəšilōh), Dan maintained its idolatrous shrine in the north. Two cult sites, one legitimate and authorized, one stolen and unauthorized — the parallel existence of covenant and apostasy across the tribal period. Shiloh's eventual destruction (1 Sam 4; Jer 7:12-14) does not close the northern shrine; the Danite idolatry outlasts the legitimate sanctuary, a testimony to how deeply apostasy takes root.
3: wayyakkîrû ʾet-qôl hannáʿar hallewî wayyāsurrû šām wayyōʾmərû lô mî hēḇîʾăkā pōh ûmah-ʾattāh ʿōśeh bāzeh ûmah-lekā pōh — 'They recognized the voice of the young Levite; they turned in there and said to him: Who brought you here, and what are you doing in this place, and what do you have here?' nākar (H5234, to recognize by distinctive characteristics) implies they know this man — a Levite priest mobile enough to hire himself out is precisely what a founding tribal cult needs. The spies' three questions ('who brought you, what are you doing, what do you have') mirror the language of criminal inquiry, hinting that the scene is already morally suspect: what is a legitimate Levite doing inside a house of syncretistic idols?
4: wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem kōh-wəkōh ʿāśāh-lî mîkāh wayyiśkərēnî wāʾehyeh-lô ləkōhēn — 'He said to them: Thus and so Micah did for me — he hired me and I am his priest.' śākar (H7936, to hire for wages) is the key verb: the Levite is a mercenary priest. The word recurs in 18:20 when he immediately upgrades to a more profitable client. Ezekiel's indictment of shepherds who fleece the flock (Ezek 34:2-4) and Jesus's contrast of hired hand and true shepherd (John 10:11-13) both draw on the same cultural fact: priestly service divorced from covenant vocation produces hireling ministry that serves the highest bidder.
5: wayyōʾmərû lô šaʾal-nāʾ bēʾlōhîm wənēdāʿāh hayiṣlîaḥ darkēnû ʾăšer ʾănaḥnû hōlkîm ʿālêhā — 'They said to him: Please inquire of God for us — we want to know whether our journey on which we are going will succeed.' šāʾal bēʾlōhîm (H7592 H430, inquire of God) is the legitimate priestly consultation idiom (cf. 1 Sam 28:6; Num 27:21). The problem is structural: the deity being consulted is mediated through stolen, man-made cult objects (17:4-5), and the oracle pronounced (v6) is favorable — YHWH's name is invoked to sanction unauthorized conquest. Authentic inquiry of YHWH required the tabernacle, the Aaronic priesthood, and Urim/Thummim; what Micah offers is a counterfeit at every level.
8: wayyāšûḇû ʾel-ʾăḥêhem Ṣorʿāh wəʾEštāʾôl wayyōʾmərû ʾălêhem ʾăḥêhem mah ʾattem — 'They returned to their relatives at Zorah and Eshtaol. Their relatives said to them: What have you to report?' These towns — Zorah and Eshtaol — are precisely where the spirit of YHWH first stirred Samson (13:25) and where he was buried (16:31). The tribe of Dan is now setting out from Samson's homeland to abandon it entirely; the judge's home territory is being vacated in favor of unauthorized conquest far to the north. The shadow of Samson's failed mission — representing Dan's failed vocation — hangs over the departure.
9: wayyōʾmərû qûmû wənáʿăleh ʿălêhem kî rāʾînû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ wəhinnēh ṭôḇāh meʾōd wəʾattem maḥărîšîm ʾal-tēʾālû lirešet ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — 'They said: Arise, let us go up against them — we have seen the land and it is very good. And you are silent? Do not hesitate; go and possess the land.' ṭôḇāh meʾōd (very good) is the same phrase the Israelite spies used of Canaan in Num 14:7 — but there the good land was their divinely assigned inheritance; here it is a land God never promised Dan. The Caleb-Joshua minority report ('the land is good, go up and possess it') is being inverted and misapplied to justify unauthorized seizure.
10: ûḇəḇôʾăkem tāḇōʾû ʾel-ʿam bōṭēaḥ wəhāʾāreṣ raḥăḇat-yādayim kî nātannāh ʾĕlōhîm bəyedkem maqôm ʾăšer ʾên-šām maḥsôr kol-dāḇār ʾăšer bāʾāreṣ — 'When you arrive, you will come to a people secure, and the land is broad of hands — God has given it into your hands, a place where there is no lack of anything on earth.' bōṭēaḥ (H982, secure, trusting) — the word used for Laish's defenseless population in v7. The spies invoke divine gift-language (nātān ʾĕlōhîm bəyedkem — God has given into your hands) identical to the formula used for YHWH's authorized conquests under Joshua (Josh 2:24, 6:2, 8:1). But YHWH never spoke here; the authorization is asserted, not received.
11: wayyisʿû miššām mimmišpaḥat haddānî miṣṣorʿāh ûmēʾEštāʾôl šēš-meʾôt ʾîš ḥāgûr kəlê milḥāmāh — 'From there of the family of Dan, from Zorah and Eshtaol, six hundred men set out, girded with weapons of war.' šēš-meʾôt (600) echoes the 600 Benjaminite survivors who fled to the rock of Rimmon (20:47) and the 600 Philistine lords (3:31 — Shamgar). The number marks a tribal militia, not a divinely mustered army. In Joshua's campaigns Israel went out explicitly commanded and led by YHWH; here 600 men self-mobilize, armed, with stolen cult objects as their standard. The absence of divine commissioning is the structural critique.
12: wayyaʿălû wayyaḥănû bəqiryat Yeʿarîm biyhûḏāh ʿal-kēn qārəʾû lammāqôm hahûʾ Maḥănēh-Dān ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh hinnēh ʾaḥărê qiryat-yeʿārîm — 'They went up and camped at Kiriath-jearim in Judah. Therefore they called that place Mahaneh-dan (Camp of Dan) to this day; behold, it is behind Kiriath-jearim.' The place-name Maḥănēh-Dān (Camp of Dan) was already used in 13:25 for a place where the Spirit stirred Samson. The narrator's double use of this place-name links Samson's failed career as Dan's judge with the tribe's subsequent apostasy: both uses of the name mark Dan's inability to occupy its territory through the Spirit and covenant means.
13: wayyaʿaḇrû miššām har-ʾEprayim wayyāḇōʾû ʿaḏ-bêt mîkāh — 'They passed from there to the hill country of Ephraim and came to the house of Micah.' The narrative unfolds with deliberate geographical precision: the 600 armed Danites are closing in on Micah's house, whose location has been established for the reader. The moral logic is anticipatory: what was established as a private cult (ch17) will now be stolen and nationalized as a tribal cult. Private syncretism scales to public apostasy.
15: wayyāsurrû šām wayyāḇōʾû ʾel-bêt hannáʿar hallewî bêt mîkāh wayyišʾălû-lô ləšālôm — 'They turned aside there and came to the house of the young Levite at the house of Micah, and greeted him.' šāʾal ləšālôm (H7592 H7965, ask for peace/welfare — a greeting) uses the same root as the oracle consultation of v5: šāʾal bēʾlōhîm. The Levite's hospitality greeting is now the prelude to his recruitment. The five spies who previously used him for a favorable oracle are now returning to negotiate his employment transfer — using the social courtesy of greeting to disarm him before presenting the offer.
16: wəhašš ēš-meʾôt ʾîš ḥăgûrê kəlê milḥāmāh nitsṣāḇîm petaḥ-haššáʿar ʾăšer mibbnê-dān — 'And the six hundred men girded with weapons of war from the sons of Dan were standing at the entrance of the gate.' The 600 armed soldiers at the gate constitute silent intimidation: the five negotiators inside are backed by overwhelming force outside. The hospitality setting (house, greeting, Levite) is undercut by the armed men at the threshold. This is coercive negotiation dressed as social visit — the structural template for how the powerful extract compliance from the weak throughout Judges.
17: wayyaʿălû ḥămiššāt hāʾănāšîm hahōlkîm lərāggēl ʾet-hāʾāreṣ bāʾû šāmmāh lāqəḥû ʾet-happεsel wəʾet-hāʾēpôd wəʾet-hattərāpîm wəʾet-hamassēkāh wəhakkōhēn nitsṣāḇ petaḥ haššáʿar — 'The five men who had gone to scout the land went up, entered, and took the carved image, the ephod, the teraphim, and the molten image; the priest was standing at the gate entrance.' lāqaḥ (H3947, to take) is the standard appropriation verb; no euphemism — they take. The fourfold object list (pesel, ʾēpôd, tərāpîm, massēkāh) is identical to the inventory description in 17:4-5, emphasizing the complete transfer of Micah's cult apparatus. The priest is watching from the gate — he is complicit in the theft by proximity and silence.
18: ûḵəḇôʾ ʾēlleh ʾel-bêt mîkāh wayyiqqəḥû ʾet-pesel hâʾĕpôd wəʾet-hattərāpîm wəʾet-hamassēkāh wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem hakkōhēn mah ʾattem ʿōśîm — 'When these men entered Micah's house and took the carved image, the ephod, the teraphim, and the molten image, the priest said to them: What are you doing?' The priest's one-sentence protest — mah ʾattem ʿōśîm (what are you doing?) — is notable for its weakness. He does not resist, does not shout for help, does not invoke YHWH. His question is rhetorical prelude to the offer he is already inclined to accept (v20). The protest functions as face-saving hesitation before capitulation, not moral objection.
20: wayyîṭaḇ lēḇ hakkōhēn wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-hāʾēpôd wəʾet-hattərāpîm wəʾet-happεsel wayyābōʾ bəqereḇ hāʿam — 'The priest's heart was glad, and he took the ephod and the teraphim and the carved image and went in the midst of the people.' wayyîṭaḇ lēḇ (H3190 H3820, his heart was glad) — the same phrase used ironically in 19:6 for the ill-fated hospitality scene and in 16:25 when the Philistines fetched Samson for sport. Gladness here is opportunism: the Levite abandons his employer Micah without ceremony and immediately integrates into the Danite formation. He did not need to be coerced; he was waiting for a better offer. The hireling transfers his loyalty to the highest bidder.
21: wayyāśîmû ʾet-haṭṭap wəʾet-hamiqneh wəʾet-hakəḇûdāh lipnêhem wayyēlkû — 'They put the little ones and the livestock and the goods before them and went.' The tactical arrangement — children, animals, and cargo in front — protects the column from rear attack (v22). kəḇûdāh (H3520, goods, heavy possessions — from kāḇēd, heavy/glorious) is the same root as kāḇôd (glory). The Danites are marching with their 'glory' — their stolen religious apparatus and material wealth — but it is the false glory of idols, not the kəḇôd YHWH that guided Israel in the wilderness. The entire formation parodies the Israelite wilderness march.
22: hēmmāh hirḥîqû mibêt mîkāh wəhāʾănāšîm ʾăšer babbāttîm ʾăšer ʿim-bêt-mîkāh niṣʿăqû wayyaddəbîqû ʾet-bənê-Dān — 'They had distanced themselves from the house of Micah when the men in the houses near Micah's house cried out and overtook the sons of Dan.' niṣʿăqû (H6817, cried out) — the cry for help; the same root used when Israel cried to YHWH under oppression (3:9, 4:3, 6:6). Here the cry is unanswered because there is no YHWH in this scene: no covenant, no judge, no deliverance. Micah's community can pursue but cannot recover; the Danites are beyond accountability.
23: wayyipnû bənê-Dān ʾel-mîkāh wayyōʾmərû mah-lăkā kî niṣʿāqtā — 'The sons of Dan turned to Micah and said: What is wrong with you that you have cried out?' The callousness of the question is rhetorically precise: the Danites do not say 'we have done nothing wrong' — they question the propriety of Micah's complaint. The powerful dismiss the grievance of the weak by questioning their right to protest. Micah's answer (v24) is one of the most theologically pointed lines in Judges: ʾet-ʾĕlōhay ʾăšer ʿāśîtî laqaḥtem — you have taken my gods which I made — exposing the idolatry as self-made religion, yet still called 'my gods.'
24: wayyōʾmer ʾet-ʾĕlōhay ʾăšer ʿāśîtî laqaḥtem wəʾet-hakkōhēn wattelkû ûmah-lî ʿôd ûmah-zeh tōʾmərû ʾēlay — 'He said: You have taken my gods which I made, and the priest, and you have gone. What more do I have? And what is this you say to me?' The phrase ʾĕlōhay ʾăšer ʿāśîtî (my gods which I made) is the narrator's devastating verdict embedded in Micah's own mouth. The man who made his gods cannot keep them — they have no power to protect themselves or him. Isaiah 44:9-20 and Jeremiah 10:3-5 develop this logic explicitly: the idol-maker exhausts himself making what cannot save him. Micah's lament is an unwitting theological confession.
25: wayyōʾmərû ʾēlāyw bənê-Dān ʾal-titten qôlkā ʿimmānû pen-yippəgʿû bākem ʾănāšîm marrê-nepeš wəʾāsaptā napšekā ûbêt bêtekā — 'The sons of Dan said to him: Do not let your voice be heard among us, lest men bitter of soul attack you and you lose your life and your household.' marrê-nepeš (H4751 H5315, bitter/hot of soul) — men in desperate or violent disposition; the term is used of Hannah (1 Sam 1:10), the men of Jabesh-gilead (1 Sam 22:2), and Naomi (Ruth 1:20). The threat is naked: silence or death. This is the language of the powerful coercing the powerless. The Danites' martial prowess — celebrated as national expansion — is here exposed as organized intimidation.
26: wayyēlkû bənê-Dān lədarkām wəmîkāh rāʾāh kî-ḥăzāqîm hēmmāh mimmennû wayyāpεn wayyāšāḇ ʾel-bêtô — 'The sons of Dan went their way. When Micah saw that they were stronger (ḥāzāq) than he, he turned and went back to his house.' ḥāzaq (H2389, strong, mighty) is the word YHWH speaks over Joshua: ḥăzaq wêʾĕmāṣ (be strong and courageous, Josh 1:6-9). Here it describes the Danites' superior force against an individual. Strength without covenant authorization is merely power — the same raw force the Canaanite kings exercised, now replicated by an Israelite tribe. The Danites have become what they were supposed to displace.
28: wəʾên maṣṣîl kî rəḥôqāh hîʾ miṣṣîḏôn wûdāḇār ʾên-lāhem ʿim-ʾāḏām wəhîʾ bāʿēmeq ʾăšer lêBêt-Rəḥôḇ wayyibnû ʾet-hāʿîr wayyēšəḇû ḇāh — 'There was no deliverer (maṣṣîl) because it was far from Sidon and had no dealings with anyone; it was in the valley belonging to Beth-rehob. They built the city and settled in it.' maṣṣîl (H5337, Hiphil ptc. of nāṣal, to deliver/rescue) — precisely the term applied to YHWH as Israel's deliverer throughout Judges (2:16, 2:18). The people of Laish had no human deliverer because they had no covenant community; Israel has a deliverer-God who redeems the oppressed. Judges' irony: the covenant people become the oppressors of the defenseless.
29: wayyiqrəʾû ʾet-šem-hāʿîr Dān bəšem Dān ʾăḇîhem ʾăšer yullad ləyiśrāʾēl ʾûlām Lāyiš šem-hāʿîr lāriʾšōnāh — 'They called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father who was born to Israel; but Laish was the name of the city formerly.' The naming inverts the positive pattern of divinely authorized name-giving. Jacob named Dan's mother's exclamation: dānannî ʾĕlōhîm (God has judged/vindicated me, Gen 30:6) — Dan = God-has-vindicated. Here Dan-the-city bears the name of God's vindication over a conquest God never authorized and over a people who had no part in Israel's covenant. The name becomes ironic: where is God's judgment in this scene? Jeroboam will later install a golden calf at this very city (1 Kgs 12:29).
